segments.text: I'm Chris Montero, I'm the president of the Cambridge Education Association.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And welcome to the public, including caregivers and educators and students of Cambridge, as well as the candidates who have joined us here tonight.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: This is the second CEA school committee candidate forum.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: and we are excited to host this opportunity to engage in active democracy.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: It's important to have these community events because we often see democratic norms and participation under attack these days, especially from public officials.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: By being here, we are all showing that this kind of democratic engagement still matters and that people care about their public schools.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So thank you for being here.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: To start, each candidate will take one minute to introduce themselves.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Then we have prepared some questions from the CEA membership.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Candidates,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: you will need to hold your responses to a minute unless otherwise noted.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: We are going to let you know when you have used up 45 seconds and have 15 seconds left.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: April is gonna give you a wave.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And then when you've hit your 60 seconds full minute, you're gonna get one of these.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: from April, and then if you keep going after that, then you gotta deal with me or Sarah Urgell.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So please try to keep it down, that way we can get to all of the questions we have.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Also a note that not all candidates will answer all questions.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So we will clarify before we read the questions who it's directed to.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: towards the end of the program we will take questions from the audience audience we are going to circulate with index cards and pens throughout the program so if you have questions that you want to ask you can jot them down and then you can just raise that index card up and our folks will come by to grab it from you and then we will have closing statements of about a minute
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So that's it for the intro.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Again, welcome to everybody who is just coming in.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And we are going to go ahead and get started with our first question.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And I'm going to go ahead and direct it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: It's going to be to everyone.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And again, you have one minute to answer this question.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: How will you ensure that families with disproportionate influence do not drown out the needs of underrepresented?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Oh yeah.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I just said we were gonna do opening statements.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Thank you guys.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: That was a test and you all passed.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: A plus.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: We will start with opening statements of one minute each, beginning with member Weinstein.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Great, thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: All right.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Hi, I'm David Weinstein.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I ask you for your number one vote to re-elect me to build on what I've accomplished.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I've lived in Cambridge for 24 years, and I'm a proud Cambridge Public Schools parent of an eighth grader and a 2025 CRLS graduate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: My commitment to education begins with my own public school experience.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I did well academically, loved my teachers, made lifelong friends, things that I wish for every child, but I could see disparities and opportunity in my diverse hometown.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I became a teacher.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I taught in public elementary and high schools, in a special education summer camp, in a school for the deaf, and have spent my career in K-12 and college education.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I research teacher leadership and policy at the Harvard Grad School of Education, and I've guided and grown a 50-state civic education program based at Brandeis University.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I know how to connect innovations and ideals with classroom and community realities.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: We have so much in Cambridge.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Incredible children, dedicated and talented teachers and staff, wonderful families, innovative city programs, community partners, and a strong budget.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: We do so much so well, yet some children still fall through the cracks and disparities persist.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: My service on the school committee has been dedicated to solving this contradiction.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I'm proud of the Cambridge schools, but not satisfied.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Hi, everybody.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I'm not going to talk about me because I don't matter.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: What matters is that we can deliver for you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And to that end, oh, hi, I'm Elizabeth Hudson.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Sorry, I should have said that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Yeah, that was an important point of view, important detail.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So I'm Elizabeth Hudson.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I'm not going to talk about myself because you could read about me elsewhere.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But I'm going to talk about how we deliver for you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: All right.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: First point is that I don't play fast and loose with budget numbers or policy details.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: The budget is a public document, by the way, and everybody has these numbers available now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So when we talk about this evening, things like paraprofessional pay or whether or not schools are fully funded, ask people for specific details.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Ask what those numbers actually are and ask how they'll actually pay for them.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Those are details that they should have available.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And I also have a spine.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: You can get elected with nice words, but you can't educate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01
